does: This paper identifies pedagogical jailbreaks—where students prompt educational LLMs to give direct answers rather than teaching scaffolds—and proposes SHAPE, a unified framework addressing safety, helpfulness, and pedagogy in tutoring systems. The authors formalize these three dimensions using a knowledge-mastery graph, introduce a benchmark of 9,087 student-question pairs to evaluate tutoring behavior under adversarial conditions, and develop a graph-augmented pipeline that infers prerequisite concepts, detects mastery gaps, and explicitly gates between instructional versus problem-solving responses. Experiments across multiple LLMs demonstrate improved robustness to pedagogical jailbreaks while preserving helpfulness.
connects: This work extends prior research on prompt injection attacks and jailbreaks in LLMs by identifying a domain-specific vulnerability in educational contexts. It builds on existing safety evaluation frameworks by incorporating pedagogical objectives typically studied separately in intelligent tutoring systems literature. The paper combines classical knowledge graph reasoning from educational technology with modern LLM alignment techniques, responding to growing concerns about LLM robustness in high-stakes domains. It also connects to work on value alignment and multi-objective optimization in language models, where conflicting objectives like safety and utility must be jointly satisfied.
enables: This framework opens pathways for developing more robust educational AI systems that balance safety with pedagogical effectiveness. Future work could extend the approach to other specialized domains where adversarial prompts pose distinct risks tied to domain-specific objectives. The explicit knowledge-mastery graph mechanism could inform more general methods for decomposing and enforcing complex behavioral constraints in LLMs through structured reasoning. The benchmark enables systematic evaluation of tutoring alignment, potentially catalyzing development of evaluation standards for educational AI similar to existing safety benchmarks. The gating mechanism might generalize to other multi-objective scenarios requiring dynamic behavior routing based on context and adversarial pressure.
